sections.paragraph:
f., a word of the later Hebrew.

(1) fortress, castle, palace (see below the Ch. and Syr. If the word be Phœnicio-Shemitic, it may have come from אֲבִירָה strong, fortified, or as others prefer, נְבִירָה, from the Ethiop. root ነበረ፡ to sit; whence መንበር፡ a throne, a tribunal, and مِنْبَرُ throne, tribunal, metropolis, as if royal seat. Perhaps however, the word is of Persic origin, compare Pers. بارو baru, fortress, wall, castle, Sanscr. bura, buri, pur, Greek πύργος and βάρις). There often occurs שׁוּשַׁן הַבִּירָה Shushan, the palace, almost always used of the royal abode, Neh 1:1 Est 1:2 2:3 , 3:15 Dan 8:2 but also of the whole adjoining city, Est 1:5 2:5 8:14 9:6 , 11 , 12 (compare Ezr 6:2); which is elsewhere more accurately called הָעִיר שׁוּשַׁן ( Est 3:15 8:15 ). When applied to Jerusalem, the fortress of the temple is meant, Neh 2:8.

(2) a temple, 1Ch 29:1, 19 1Ch 29:19.

בירה

Ch. f. emphat. בִּירְתָּא id., fortress, palace, Ezr 6:2 Syr. ܒܺܝܪܬܐܳ.
